questions.question: Define an interrupt and explain why interrupts are preferred over polling in microcontroller systems.
questions.hint: Compare the two approaches and emphasize efficiency
keyPoints: Interrupt is a signal to processor indicating an event requiring immediate attention
Processor suspends current execution, saves state, and executes ISR
Polling wastes CPU cycles by continuously checking device status
Interrupts provide efficient CPU utilization
Interrupts enable faster response to critical events
Interrupts simplify software design for asynchronous events

questions.question: Differentiate between hardware interrupts and software interrupts (traps) with suitable examples.
questions.hint: Focus on the source and timing characteristics
keyPoints: Hardware interrupts are generated by external hardware devices
Software interrupts are triggered by software instructions (SWI)
Hardware interrupts are asynchronous to program execution
Software interrupts are synchronous - occur at specific program points
Examples: Keyboard press, timer (hardware); system calls, debugging (software)
Software interrupts are also called traps or exceptions

questions.question: Explain the difference between maskable and non-maskable interrupts (NMI) in ARM processors.
questions.hint: Mention how each is handled in the CPSR register
keyPoints: Maskable interrupts can be temporarily disabled by the processor
NMI cannot be masked or ignored by the processor
Maskable interrupts use IRQ line in ARM
NMI uses FIQ line in ARM for highest priority
NMI used for critical events like power failure, hardware fault
Interrupt mask bits in CPSR control masking in ARM

questions.question: Explain the terms 'Interrupt Latency' and 'Interrupt Response Time' in the context of microcontroller interrupt handling.
questions.hint: Distinguish between the two related but different concepts
keyPoints: Interrupt latency is time from interrupt request to start of ISR execution
Interrupt response time is time from IRQ assertion to first instruction of ISR
Latency affected by: current processor state, interrupt enable, instruction in progress
FIQ has lower latency than IRQ in ARM processors
Lower latency crucial for real-time applications
Methods to reduce latency: priority assignment, interrupt nesting, faster memory

questions.question: Explain the role of the Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) in ARM processors. Why is it typically placed at a fixed memory address?
keyPoints: IVT contains addresses of exception handlers for different interrupt types
Each interrupt source has a dedicated entry in the vector table
ARM vector table located at fixed address 0x00000000 (or 0xFFFF0000)
Fixed location allows CPU to quickly locate handlers without searching
Vector table includes: Reset, Undefined instruction, SWI, Prefetch abort, Data abort, IRQ, FIQ
Enables fast, deterministic interrupt dispatch

questions.question: Compare IRQ and FIQ exceptions in ARM processors, highlighting their differences in priority and usage.
questions.hint: Focus on performance and priority differences
keyPoints: IRQ (Interrupt Request) is normal priority interrupt
FIQ (Fast Interrupt Request) is highest priority interrupt
FIQ has dedicated register bank (r8-r12) for faster context switching
FIQ can preempt IRQ handling
IRQ shared among multiple devices; FIQ typically for single critical device
FIQ handler can execute directly without banked register save overhead

questions.question: Describe the complete sequence of steps involved in processing a hardware interrupt from the time it occurs to the resumption of the main program. Include the role of the processor, vector table, and ISR.
questions.hint: Cover both processor actions and software actions
keyPoints: External device asserts IRQ/FIQ line
Processor completes current instruction being executed
Processor saves current CPSR to SPSR and PC to LR
Processor switches to corresponding exception mode (IRQ/FIQ mode)
Processor disables further interrupts by setting I-bit in CPSR
Processor loads PC with vector address from vector table
Vector table entry branches to actual ISR
ISR executes - saves registers, handles interrupt, restores registers
ISR returns using SUBS PC, LR, #4 (or equivalent)
Processor restores CPSR from SPSR and PC from link register
Normal program execution resumes

questions.question: Explain the various exception modes in ARM processor and the purpose of banked registers in exception handling.
questions.hint: List all modes and explain why banked registers are essential
keyPoints: ARM has 7 exception modes: User, System, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, IRQ, FIQ
Each exception mode has its own banked registers (r13, r14, SPSR)
Banked r13 (SP) provides separate stack for each mode
Banked r14 (LR) stores return address for exception handler
SPSR saves CPSR for return to previous mode
User and System share same registers; other modes are privileged
Banked registers enable fast context switch without memory access
Mode changes occur automatically on exception entry

questions.question: Design a basic interrupt handling system for a microcontroller with 4 interrupt sources. Explain the components including priority assignment, vector table, and ISR structure.
questions.hint: Include both hardware and software design aspects
keyPoints: Use priority encoder for 4 interrupt sources
Assign priorities: Source 0 (highest), Source 1, Source 2, Source 3 (lowest)
Vector table entries at fixed addresses for each interrupt
Each source has dedicated ISR entry point
ISR structure: save context, identify source, handle interrupt, restore context
Use global variable to track pending interrupts if using shared vector
Interrupt controller handles priority and enables/disables individual sources
Stack design: allocate separate stack for IRQ mode
Consider nested interrupts if high-priority interrupts must interrupt lower ones

questions.question: Compare interrupt-driven I/O with Direct Memory Access (DMA). Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other?
questions.hint: Consider the trade-offs in terms of CPU utilization and complexity
keyPoints: Interrupt-driven I/O: CPU involved in each data transfer, generates interrupt per operation
DMA: Data transferred directly between device and memory without CPU intervention
Interrupt-driven: suitable for low data volume, simple devices
DMA: suitable for high-volume data transfer (disk, network, audio)
DMA reduces CPU overhead and interrupt frequency
DMA adds hardware complexity and cost
DMA provides faster data transfer for bulk operations
Choice depends on: data volume, CPU utilization requirement, hardware cost, latency needs

questions.question: Explain the concept of nested interrupts in ARM processors. What are the considerations for implementing nested interrupt handling?
questions.hint: Explain the stack and register saving considerations
keyPoints: Nested interrupts allow higher priority interrupt to interrupt lower priority ISR
Requires explicit enable of interrupts within ISR (using CPSIE I)
Must save SPSR and restore correctly when nesting
Stack must be large enough to hold multiple ISR contexts
Risk of stack overflow if too many nested levels
Priority inversion must be carefully managed
Nested interrupts increase interrupt latency for lower priority interrupts
ARM banked registers simplify nested handling but care needed with LR

questions.question: Calculate the maximum interrupt latency for an ARM processor given: Clock frequency = 50 MHz, longest instruction takes 3 cycles, maximum pipeline depth = 5 stages, and interrupts are disabled for at most 4 cycles during mode change.
questions.hint: Sum all delay components in the interrupt response path
keyPoints: Time per cycle = 1 / 50 MHz = 20 ns
Longest instruction time = 3 × 20 ns = 60 ns
Pipeline flush penalty = 5 × 20 ns = 100 ns
Mode change overhead = 4 × 20 ns = 80 ns
Vector fetch and branch = approximately 2-3 cycles = 40-60 ns
Maximum latency = 60 + 100 + 80 + 60 = 300 ns (approximately)
Actual latency depends on instruction at interrupt time

questions.question: A microcontroller has 3 interrupt sources with the following worst-case ISR execution times: Source A: 50 μs, Source B: 100 μs, Source C: 200 μs. If interrupts occur at rates: A: 1000/sec, B: 500/sec, C: 200/sec, calculate the total CPU utilization due to interrupt processing.
questions.hint: Multiply each ISR time by its frequency
keyPoints: CPU time for Source A = 50 μs × 1000 = 50 ms/sec
CPU time for Source B = 100 μs × 500 = 50 ms/sec
CPU time for Source C = 200 μs × 200 = 40 ms/sec
Total interrupt CPU time = 50 + 50 + 40 = 140 ms/sec
Total CPU utilization = (140 / 1000) × 100% = 14%
CPU has 86% time available for main program
Check if this is sustainable considering interrupt overhead

questions.question: Design an interrupt priority scheme for a system with: Timer (periodic, 1ms), UART (variable data rate, 115200 bps), GPIO button (user input), and ADC (sensor sampling at 10 kHz). Assign priorities and justify your choices.
questions.hint: Consider the data loss consequences and timing requirements
keyPoints: ADC: Highest priority (Priority 1) - 10 kHz sampling requires consistent timing
Timer: High priority (Priority 2) - System tick determines timing accuracy
GPIO: Medium priority (Priority 3) - User input needs responsive handling
UART: Lower priority (Priority 4) - Can tolerate some delay, buffered data
Justification: ADC data loss unacceptable; Timer critical for system; GPIO impacts user experience; UART has buffers
Consider using FIQ for ADC if available
Implement nesting if needed for critical multiple simultaneous interrupts
